paragraphs.text: Here’s one neat SRD-46-supported rare-earth pattern: **lanthanides bind EDTA more strongly as you move from light to heavy rare earths**.

claims.text: **lanthanides bind EDTA more strongly as you move from light to heavy rare earths**

paragraphs.text: Using **EDTA (ligand_6277; Ethylenedinitrilotetraacetic acid)**, SRD-46 shows measured **logK** data for multiple trivalent rare-earth ions at **25 °C** and **ionic strength 0.1–1 M**. Coverage includes La, Ce, Pr, Nd, Sm, Eu, Gd, Tb, Dy, Ho, Er, and Lu.

paragraphs.text: What’s interesting is the **trend itself**: SRD-46 has enough matched EDTA measurements across the series to show the classic **lanthanide contraction** effect. As ionic radius shrinks from **La³⁺ to Lu³⁺**, the metal ion generally fits the chelator better and forms a stronger complex.

claims.text: SRD-46 has enough matched EDTA measurements across the series
to show the classic **lanthanide contraction** effect.
As ionic radius shrinks from **La³⁺ to Lu³⁺**,
the metal ion generally fits the chelator better

grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: Established chemistry: lanthanide ionic radii decrease from La3+ to Lu3+ (lanthanide contraction); not a specific SRD-46 row.
General rationale: smaller Ln3+ typically fit multidentate chelators (like EDTA) more snugly; this is domain knowledge, not a database-stated fact.
General trend in coordination chemistry: along Ln3+ series, decreasing radius often increases complex stability with EDTA; not directly evidenced by provided rows.

paragraphs.text:
| Observation | SRD-46 support |
|---|---|
| Light rare earths are represented | La³⁺, Ce³⁺, Pr³⁺, Nd³⁺ present with logK data |
| Middle rare earths are represented | Sm³⁺, Eu³⁺, Gd³⁺, Tb³⁺, Dy³⁺ present with logK data |
| Heavy rare earths are represented | Ho³⁺, Er³⁺, Lu³⁺ present with logK data |
| Thermodynamic depth exists beyond logK | SRD-46 also contains ΔH and ΔS entries for many of these EDTA complexes |

paragraphs.text: A second cool point: this is not just a single-number dataset. For these EDTA–rare-earth systems, SRD-46 also reports **ΔH** and **ΔS** for many ions, so you can look not only at “how strong” the binding is, but also whether the driving force is more **enthalpic** or **entropic** across the series.

claims.text: this is not just a single-number dataset.
For these EDTA–rare-earth systems, SRD-46 also reports **ΔH** and **ΔS** for many ions,
so you can look not only at “how strong” the binding is,
but also whether the driving force is more **enthalpic** or **entropic** across the series.
